comment_text.sn4.7:2.1: Lacking substantive arguments, Māra picks on the Buddha for sleeping.

root_text.sn4.7:1.1: Ekaṁ samayaṁ bhagavā rājagahe viharati veḷuvane kalandakanivāpe.
root_text.sn4.7:1.2: Atha kho bhagavā bahudevarattiṁ abbhokāse caṅkamitvā rattiyā paccūsasamayaṁ pāde pakkhāletvā vihāraṁ pavisitvā
root_text.sn4.7:1.3: dakkhiṇena passena sīhaseyyaṁ kappesi pāde pādaṁ accādhāya sato sampajāno uṭṭhānasaññaṁ manasi karitvā.
root_text.sn4.7:1.4: Atha kho māro pāpimā yena bhagavā tenupasaṅkami; upasaṅkamitvā bhagavantaṁ gāthāya ajjhabhāsi:

translation_text.sn4.7:1.1: At one time the Buddha was staying near Rājagaha, in the Bamboo Grove, the squirrels’ feeding ground.
translation_text.sn4.7:1.2: He spent much of the night walking mindfully in the open. At the crack of dawn he washed his feet and entered his dwelling.
translation_text.sn4.7:1.3: He laid down in the lion’s posture—on the right side, placing one foot on top of the other—mindful and aware, and focused on the time of getting up.
translation_text.sn4.7:1.4: Then Māra the Wicked went up to the Buddha and addressed him in verse:
translation_text.sn4.7:2.2: You sleep like a loser—<j>what’s up with that?
translation_text.sn4.7:2.3: You sleep, thinking that the hut is empty.
translation_text.sn4.7:2.4: You sleep when the sun has come up—<j>what’s up with that?”
translation_text.sn4.7:3.2: the weaver, the clinger—<j>to track them anywhere.
translation_text.sn4.7:3.3: With the ending of all attachments <j>the awakened Buddha sleeps.
translation_text.sn4.7:3.4: What’s that got to do with you, Māra?”
